nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich areas of inner lagoons, subtidal reef flats and seaward reefs, where adults establish stationary cleaning stations visited by client fish.

decorPreferences: soft sandy substrate (rough gravel can abrade fins)
well-established reef system with diverse host-fish community

diet.notes: In the wild, the Cleaner Wrasse feeds almost exclusively on ectoparasites — gnathiid isopods and copepods — as well as mucus and dead scales picked from client fish that visit its established cleaning stations. In captivity, most specimens refuse prepared foods entirely and slowly starve, because no home aquarium can supply the steady stream of parasite-laden clients the fish depends on. The few individuals that do adapt are generally coaxed onto vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and live black worms, offered in very small portions several times a day. Even adapted individuals tend to remain dependent on grazing living tankmates for a substantial portion of their nutrition.

goodWith: large peaceful community fish that provide parasite-grazing opportunities
avoidWith: conspecifics (other Labroides) in tanks under ~700 L
large predators such as lionfish, frogfish, groupers and morays
very small invertebrates that could be mistaken for parasites (occasional clam-mantle picking has been reported)

imageCandidates.notes: Elias Levy; 2297x1722; tack-sharp diagonal shot of a lone adult, vivid electric-blue dorsal edging and forked tail over a clean white body with the bold black lateral stripe broadening toward the tail - textbook diagnostic features.
Diego Delso (Red Sea, Egypt); 3098x2065; crisp full lateral profile on a dark background, whole fish in frame with electric-blue caudal and clear white belly/black stripe.
Diego Delso (Oman); 5221x3481; high-res, intensely glowing neon-blue lateral streak - very striking colour; belly slightly shadowed, kept as an alternate.

summary: The Cleaner Wrasse is a reef-dwelling Indo-Pacific species that makes its living removing parasites and mucus from other fish at established cleaning stations. Despite its distinctive coloration and fascinating behavior, it is widely considered one of the most difficult marine species to keep alive in home aquaria.
careNotes: ETHICAL CARE WARNING: the bluestreak cleaner wrasse is one of the most-documented welfare problems in the marine hobby. Most wild-collected specimens refuse prepared foods and die of starvation within weeks to a few months because their wild diet of ectoparasites grazed across many client fish cannot be replicated in a home tank. Many marine livestock advocates and reputable retailers discourage purchase, and on a reef they are far more valuable left in place serving multiple fish at a cleaning station than in any one private aquarium. If kept anyway, success generally requires a large, mature reef (around 400 L / ~110 gal or larger) that is heavily stocked with peaceful client fish so the wrasse can keep grazing, multiple very small feedings per day of vitamin-enriched mysis and brine shrimp and finely minced seafood, a soft sandy substrate (rough gravel can damage fins), and a tight-fitting lid as the species has been known to jump. Adults can be territorial toward conspecifics; pairs or harems should only be attempted in very large systems. Labroides dimidiatus is a protogynous hermaphrodite (female-first), with some literature describing bidirectional sex change — if a dominant male is lost, the largest female has been reported to take its place. Spawns pelagically in the wild but is not bred in captivity in any meaningful numbers.
breedingNotes: The Cleaner Wrasse is not bred in home aquaria. In the wild it forms monogamous pairs that spawn pelagically year-round at cleaning-station territories. The species is protogynous — females mature first — with documented bidirectional sex change; when a dominant male is lost, the largest female has been reported to take its place, a transition that completes within roughly 14 to 18 days, around 8 to 9 cm total length.

fish.breedingNotes: A pelagic spawner in the wild, the Cleaner Wrasse is not commercially bred. The species is protogynous, with reports of bidirectional sex change in which the largest female takes over as male when a dominant male is lost.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Most wild-collected Labroides dimidiatus die within weeks to months in captivity from starvation because they refuse prepared foods and cannot graze enough parasites in a home tank. Many marine-livestock advocates discourage purchase outright; only suitable for an experienced reef-keeper running a large, heavily-stocked reef who fully accepts the high mortality risk.
